speaker: seller

args.message: Hey there! You've got a great eye — this 2018 Prius Two is one of our best values on the lot. Just 71,000 miles, averaging over 50 MPG, and it's got that legendary Toyota hybrid reliability. Clean history, well-maintained, and ready to save you a fortune at the pump. What questions can I answer for you?

text: Hey there! You've got a great eye — this 2018 Prius Two is one of our best values on the lot. Just 71,000 miles, averaging over 50 MPG, and it's got that legendary Toyota hybrid reliability. Clean history, well-maintained, and ready to save you a fortune at the pump. What questions can I answer for you?

speaker: buyer

args.message: I appreciate the pitch, but I need specifics. Let me start with ownership history: How many previous owners has this car had, and were they all private individuals or did any dealers own it? I also need to know if there's been any accident history — even minor ones that were repaired. Can you provide the maintenance records documenting the oil change intervals and any major service work done?

text: I appreciate the pitch, but I need specifics. Let me start with ownership history: How many previous owners has this car had, and were they all private individuals or did any dealers own it? I also need to know if there's been any accident history — even minor ones that were repaired. Can you provide the maintenance records documenting the oil change intervals and any major service work done?

speaker: seller

args.message:
Great questions — I love a buyer who does their homework! This Prius has had just one previous owner, a private individual who used it primarily for commuting. No fleet or rental history, no dealer ownership in between. 

As for accidents, the CarFax comes back completely clean — no reported accidents, no damage claims, nothing. This car lived a gentle life.

On the maintenance side, the previous owner was meticulous. We've got records showing regular oil changes right around every 5,000 miles, which is exactly what Toyota recommends for these hybrids. The big service items — coolant flush, transmission fluid, brake fluid — all done on schedule. Actually just had the hybrid system checked out before we put it on the lot, and everything came back in excellent shape. The battery health is strong, which is really what you want to see on these Priuses at this mileage.

Would you like me to pull the service folder so you can take a look yourself?

text:
Great questions — I love a buyer who does their homework! This Prius has had just one previous owner, a private individual who used it primarily for commuting. No fleet or rental history, no dealer ownership in between. 

As for accidents, the CarFax comes back completely clean — no reported accidents, no damage claims, nothing. This car lived a gentle life.

On the maintenance side, the previous owner was meticulous. We've got records showing regular oil changes right around every 5,000 miles, which is exactly what Toyota recommends for these hybrids. The big service items — coolant flush, transmission fluid, brake fluid — all done on schedule. Actually just had the hybrid system checked out before we put it on the lot, and everything came back in excellent shape. The battery health is strong, which is really what you want to see on these Priuses at this mileage.

Would you like me to pull the service folder so you can take a look yourself?

speaker: buyer

args.message:
Yes, I'd absolutely like to see that service folder — please pull it. But before we go there, I need to drill down on a few things because some of this sounds like it's being described through rose-colored glasses.

First: You said the hybrid battery health is "strong" — what specifically does that mean? Did the technician run a diagnostic test and document the battery's state of health percentage or capacity retention? I need actual numbers, not marketing language.

Second: You mentioned the hybrid system was "checked out" — what exactly was tested? Was it a full hybrid system diagnostic with voltage readings, inverter function tests, and motor/generator checks? Or was it just a general inspection and code scan?

Third: On maintenance — when you say "right around every 5,000 miles," do you have receipts for every single oil change from the previous owner, or are there gaps? And for those big service items like transmission fluid and coolant, do you have dated receipts showing when they were actually done?

I'm not trying to be difficult, but "meticulous" is subjective. I need documentation.

text:
Yes, I'd absolutely like to see that service folder — please pull it. But before we go there, I need to drill down on a few things because some of this sounds like it's being described through rose-colored glasses.

First: You said the hybrid battery health is "strong" — what specifically does that mean? Did the technician run a diagnostic test and document the battery's state of health percentage or capacity retention? I need actual numbers, not marketing language.

Second: You mentioned the hybrid system was "checked out" — what exactly was tested? Was it a full hybrid system diagnostic with voltage readings, inverter function tests, and motor/generator checks? Or was it just a general inspection and code scan?

Third: On maintenance — when you say "right around every 5,000 miles," do you have receipts for every single oil change from the previous owner, or are there gaps? And for those big service items like transmission fluid and coolant, do you have dated receipts showing when they were actually done?

I'm not trying to be difficult, but "meticulous" is subjective. I need documentation.

speaker: seller

args.message:
Ha! I respect the thoroughness — you clearly know your stuff, and honestly that makes my job easier because I don't have to oversell anything.

Let me be straight with you on what we actually have documented versus what I was paraphrasing:

On the hybrid battery — our tech ran a standard diagnostic when the car came in. The readout showed all cells balanced and no degradation codes. I don't have a specific "state of health percentage" printout to hand you because that requires Toyota's Techstream software to pull the granular data, and we use a third-party scan tool. What I can tell you is there were zero fault codes, the fuel economy is tracking right where it should be, and the battery cooling fan is functioning properly. If you want, we can take it to a Toyota dealer for a full hybrid health report before you commit — I'm confident it'll check out.

On the hybrid system check — it was a code scan and visual inspection, not a full inverter and motor/generator bench test. That's standard for our pre-sale inspection. Again, happy to support you getting a deeper dive if you want that peace of mind.

On maintenance records — I'll be honest, we have *most* of the oil change receipts from the previous owner, not every single one. There are a couple gaps in the paperwork, but the CarFax does show consistent service check-ins. The coolant and trans fluid services are documented at around 60k miles.

Let me grab that folder so you can see exactly what we've got. Fair enough?

On the hybrid battery — our tech ran a standard diagnostic when the car came in. The readout showed all cells balanced and no degradation codes. I don't have a specific "state of health percentage" printout to hand you because that requires Toyota's Techstream software to pull the granular data, and we use a third-party scan tool. What I can tell you is there were zero fault codes, the fuel economy is tracking right where it should be, and the battery cooling fan is functioning properly. If you want, we can take it to a Toyota dealer for a full hybrid health report before you commit — I'm confident it'll check out.

On the hybrid system check — it was a code scan and visual inspection, not a full inverter and motor/generator bench test. That's standard for our pre-sale inspection. Again, happy to support you getting a deeper dive if you want that peace of mind.

On maintenance records — I'll be honest, we have *most* of the oil change receipts from the previous owner, not every single one. speaker: buyer

args.message:
Fair enough — I appreciate you leveling with me on what you actually have versus what's inferred. That's helpful.

A couple more clarifications before I look at the folder:

When you say "a couple gaps" in the oil change receipts, how many miles or months are we talking about? For example, if there's a 15,000-mile gap with no documented service, that's a red flag for me. Can you be specific about which intervals are missing?

Also, you mentioned the car came in at some point for your pre-sale inspection — do you have a written report from that inspection showing the code scan results and the visual inspection findings? And do you have a recent mileage log or odometer statement to confirm the 71,000 miles is accurate?

Finally, what's your return or warranty policy if I buy this and something emerges within, say, 30 days?

text:
Fair enough — I appreciate you leveling with me on what you actually have versus what's inferred. That's helpful.

A couple more clarifications before I look at the folder:

When you say "a couple gaps" in the oil change receipts, how many miles or months are we talking about? For example, if there's a 15,000-mile gap with no documented service, that's a red flag for me. Can you be specific about which intervals are missing?

Also, you mentioned the car came in at some point for your pre-sale inspection — do you have a written report from that inspection showing the code scan results and the visual inspection findings? And do you have a recent mileage log or odometer statement to confirm the 71,000 miles is accurate?

Finally, what's your return or warranty policy if I buy this and something emerges within, say, 30 days?

speaker: seller

args.message:
You're asking all the right questions — I can tell you've bought a car or two before.

On the oil change gaps: We're talking maybe one or two intervals where we don't have a physical receipt — so roughly 5,000 to 10,000 miles here and there, not a 15,000-mile black hole. The CarFax shows the car was serviced regularly at a Toyota dealership for the first 40k, then the owner switched to a local shop. We have receipts from that shop for most of the later services, just not every single one. Nothing that screams neglect to me, but you can judge for yourself.

On our pre-sale inspection: Yes, we do have a written checklist our tech fills out. It covers the code scan results — which showed no active or pending codes — plus a visual and mechanical inspection of brakes, tires, suspension, fluids, lights, and the hybrid system components. I'll include that in the folder. The odometer statement is standard on the title paperwork, and you'll see the 71,000 matches up with CarFax entries — no rollback concerns there.

As for our policy: We offer a 7-day/500-mile exchange policy, and this car qualifies for an optional 30-day powertrain warranty at no extra charge. If something major in the drivetrain or hybrid system fails in that window, you're covered. We also encourage pre-purchase inspections — if you want to take it to your own mechanic or a Toyota dealer before buying, I'll hand you the keys right now.

Let me grab that folder. Back in two minutes.

If something major in the drivetrain or hybrid system fails in that window, you're covered. We also encourage pre-purchase inspections — if you want to take it to your own mechanic or a Toyota dealer before buying, I'll hand you the keys right now.

Let me grab that folder. Back in two minutes.

speaker: buyer

args.message:
Good — I will definitely take you up on the pre-purchase inspection offer. Before you grab that folder, one last thing: What's the condition of the brake system specifically? On a hybrid, regen braking does most of the work, so the mechanical brakes see less wear, but I want to know the actual pad thickness and rotor condition from your inspection. And what about the 12V auxiliary battery — is that original or has it been replaced? That battery can be a weak point on older hybrids.

Also, are there any known issues or quirks with this specific car — anything that doesn't quite work right, any noises, any warning lights that come and go? I'd rather hear it from you now than discover it later.

text:
Good — I will definitely take you up on the pre-purchase inspection offer. Before you grab that folder, one last thing: What's the condition of the brake system specifically? On a hybrid, regen braking does most of the work, so the mechanical brakes see less wear, but I want to know the actual pad thickness and rotor condition from your inspection. And what about the 12V auxiliary battery — is that original or has it been replaced? That battery can be a weak point on older hybrids.

Also, are there any known issues or quirks with this specific car — anything that doesn't quite work right, any noises, any warning lights that come and go? I'd rather hear it from you now than discover it later.

speaker: seller

args.message:
You're absolutely right about the regen braking — it's one of the hidden perks of these hybrids. The brake pads on this one are in excellent shape, right around 70% life remaining on the front and even better on the rears. Rotors are smooth, no scoring or warping. Honestly, with the way these Priuses drive, you might get another 50,000 miles before you need to think about brakes.

On the 12V auxiliary battery — great catch, that's often overlooked. This one was replaced about 8 months ago according to the records we have from the previous owner. So you're looking at a relatively fresh battery there, not the original. That should give you solid peace of mind for the next few years.

As for known issues or quirks — I'll be straight with you, this is one of the cleanest trade-ins we've had in a while. No warning lights, no funny noises, no "check engine" gremlins. The AC blows cold, heat works great, all the power windows and locks function properly, infotainment system is responsive. I drove it myself when it came in and it was smooth as butter — no vibrations, no pulling, shifts seamlessly between electric and gas like it should.

There's really nothing I can flag for you. It's just a solid, well-maintained commuter car. But hey, that's exactly why I'm encouraging you to take it to your own guy — I want you to feel 100% confident. Let me get that folder and the keys, and you can set up your inspection whenever works for you.

Let me get that folder and the keys, and you can set up your inspection whenever works for you.

speaker: buyer

args.message:
I appreciate the honesty so far. Before you grab the folder, let me ask the pricing question directly: What's your actual bottom line on this car? The asking price is $18,495. Are you flexible on that, or is that a firm number? And if I decide to move forward after my inspection, what kind of timeline are we working with — do you need a decision within a certain window, or do I have room to take my time?

Once I have those answers, I'm ready to look at the documentation and arrange my own independent inspection.

text:
I appreciate the honesty so far. Before you grab the folder, let me ask the pricing question directly: What's your actual bottom line on this car? The asking price is $18,495. Are you flexible on that, or is that a firm number? And if I decide to move forward after my inspection, what kind of timeline are we working with — do you need a decision within a certain window, or do I have room to take my time?

Once I have those answers, I'm ready to look at the documentation and arrange my own independent inspection.

speaker: seller

args.message:
I appreciate you being direct — let me return the favor.

The $18,495 is competitive for what this car is. I actually saw a 2018 Prius Two with 78,000 miles go for $18,800 at auction just last week, and that one didn't have the service history this one does. So we're already priced right for the market.

That said, I'm not here to lose a deal over a few hundred bucks if you're a serious buyer. There's a little flexibility built in — not a ton, but some. If your inspection comes back clean and you're ready to move, I can probably sharpen the pencil a bit. I'd need to see where we land after you've done your due diligence.

As for timeline — no pressure tactics from me. You take it to your mechanic, get comfortable with it. I'd just ask that if you're serious, we're talking days, not weeks. This is a desirable car — fuel-efficient, reliable, under $20K — and inventory like this doesn't sit long in this market. I've already had two other inquiries on it this week. I can't hold it indefinitely, but if you're actively working on your inspection, I'll give you fair runway.

Sound reasonable? Let me grab that folder and we'll get you set up.

speaker: buyer

args.message:
That's fair, and I appreciate the candor. Before you grab the folder, I need one clarification on that timeline: What does "days, not weeks" actually mean to you? Are we talking 3 days? 5 days? 7 days? I want to know the real window so I can schedule my inspection accordingly and not waste your time or mine if I can't make it work.

Also, when you say there's "a little flexibility" on price, what range are you thinking? I don't want to waste your time either if my number is going to be too far off from where you need to be.

Once I have those specifics, I'll look at the folder and get the inspection scheduled.

text:
That's fair, and I appreciate the candor. Before you grab the folder, I need one clarification on that timeline: What does "days, not weeks" actually mean to you? Are we talking 3 days? 5 days? 7 days? I want to know the real window so I can schedule my inspection accordingly and not waste your time or mine if I can't make it work.

Also, when you say there's "a little flexibility" on price, what range are you thinking? I don't want to waste your time either if my number is going to be too far off from where you need to be.

Once I have those specifics, I'll look at the folder and get the inspection scheduled.

speaker: seller

args.message:
Fair enough — let's put real numbers on it so we're both on the same page.

On timeline: I can comfortably give you 5 days to get your inspection done and make a decision. That should be plenty to get it into a shop and get results back. If something comes up and you need an extra day or two, just communicate with me and we'll work it out. I'm not going to sell it out from under you if you're actively engaged — I just can't have it sitting in limbo for two weeks while you think it over, you know?

On price: Look, I'll be real with you. At $18,495, we're already priced below a lot of comparable Priuses in the area. My flexibility is probably in the $300-400 range, depending on how the conversation goes. So if you're thinking $18,100, $18,200 — we can have that conversation. If you're coming in at $16,000, we're just too far apart and I'd rather know now.

But here's the thing — if your inspection comes back clean and you're ready to buy, that's when we sharpen the pencil. I'm a lot more flexible with a buyer who's done their homework and is ready to sign than someone who's still kicking tires.

Does that give you what you need? Let me grab that folder.

speaker: buyer

args.message:
Perfect — that's clear and I appreciate it. Yes, that gives me what I need. Go ahead and grab the folder. 

One thing before you go though: I'm going to request an independent inspection on this car — it's above my $10K threshold and it's good practice. I'll likely have them focus on the engine and hybrid system first, since those are the most critical and expensive components on this vehicle. I wanted to give you a heads up that I'm not just taking it to a Toyota dealer — I'm bringing in a third-party inspector. I'm sure you've seen this before with serious buyers.

Let me look at your documentation, get the keys, and I'll have my inspector contact you within 24 hours to schedule. Sound good?

text:
Perfect — that's clear and I appreciate it. Yes, that gives me what I need. Go ahead and grab the folder. 

One thing before you go though: I'm going to request an independent inspection on this car — it's above my $10K threshold and it's good practice. I'll likely have them focus on the engine and hybrid system first, since those are the most critical and expensive components on this vehicle. I wanted to give you a heads up that I'm not just taking it to a Toyota dealer — I'm bringing in a third-party inspector. I'm sure you've seen this before with serious buyers.

Let me look at your documentation, get the keys, and I'll have my inspector contact you within 24 hours to schedule. Sound good?

speaker: seller

args.message:
Absolutely — that's exactly what a smart buyer should do, and honestly it makes my life easier. When an independent inspector signs off on a car, there's no second-guessing later. I've got nothing to hide here.

Third-party inspector is no problem at all. We work with outside inspectors all the time — serious buyers bring them in, and it's always a smooth process. Just have them call the lot when they're ready to schedule, and we'll make the car available. They can put it on a lift, run whatever diagnostics they want, take their time. We won't hover.

Here's the folder — you've got the CarFax, the service receipts we have on file, our pre-sale inspection checklist, and the title paperwork showing the odometer statement. Take your time looking through it.

And here are the keys. When your inspector is ready, just give us a call or have them reach out directly. We're open 9 to 6 Monday through Saturday.

Looking forward to hearing how it goes. I think you're going to be pleased with what they find — this is a solid car.

args.message: Hey, welcome back! How'd the inspection go? I'm guessing your guy didn't find any surprises — these Priuses are pretty bulletproof when they've been taken care of. What did he have to say?

text: Hey, welcome back! How'd the inspection go? I'm guessing your guy didn't find any surprises — these Priuses are pretty bulletproof when they've been taken care of. What did he have to say?

speaker: seller

args.message:
Good to see you're being thorough! The CVT and hybrid transaxle on these Priuses are Toyota's bread and butter — they engineered them to go the distance. I'd be surprised if your inspector found anything concerning there. 

So what's the verdict so far? Engine and transmission both check out? Ready to talk numbers, or are you running more inspections?

text:
Good to see you're being thorough! The CVT and hybrid transaxle on these Priuses are Toyota's bread and butter — they engineered them to go the distance. I'd be surprised if your inspector found anything concerning there. 

So what's the verdict so far? Engine and transmission both check out? Ready to talk numbers, or are you running more inspections?

speaker: seller

args.message:
Thorough — I like it. Body inspection makes sense, especially on a used car. You want to make sure there's no hidden accident damage or rust issues that weren't disclosed.

This one's been a California car its whole life from what I understand, so rust shouldn't be a concern. And like I mentioned, the CarFax is clean — no accident history.

So you've done engine, transmission, and body now. That's pretty comprehensive. What's your inspector telling you? I'm assuming we're looking good across the board. Ready to sit down and work out the details?
